user_query:
I’m trying to be a responsible teen without drowning in dusty manuals. Please set up and run a small, reproducible local pipeline that pulls a quick “signal” from official sources to see which basic home-maintenance chores actually show up on .gov sites, then summarize what I should prioritize. Use the baseline tasks in input/baseline_tasks.json.

Requirements
1) Download snapshots from the Internet (no search engine needed): fetch the root page HTML for these official domains: epa.gov, energy.gov, ready.gov. Save each raw HTML file under web/raw/ as:
   - web/raw/epa.gov.html
   - web/raw/energy.gov.html
   - web/raw/ready.gov.html

2) Extract and normalize text: for each snapshot, strip tags/scripts/styles and write a plain-text version to web/text/ with the same base name (e.g., web/text/epa.gov.txt). Lowercase the text for analysis.

3) Keyword counting tied to my baseline: for every keyword listed in input/baseline_tasks.json, count its case-insensitive occurrences in each domain’s normalized text (a simple, lowercase substring approach is fine). Produce:
   - analytics/keyword_counts.json with structure: {"epa.gov": {"keyword": count, ...}, "energy.gov": {...}, "ready.gov": {...}}. Use the exact keyword strings from the baseline file as keys and non-negative integers as values.
   - analytics/task_scores.csv with header: task_id,task_name,frequency,total_matches,domains_with_hits. total_matches is the sum of that task’s keyword counts across all three domains; domains_with_hits is a semicolon-separated list of domains where the task has any matches. Sort rows by total_matches descending, then task_id ascending.

4) Status summary: compose a concise report at outputs/maintenance_status.md with these sections in order:
   - Sources: list the three domains processed.
   - Fetch results: for each domain, say OK if its raw HTML file exists and is non-empty; otherwise FAILED.
   - Keyword signal (by domain): link to analytics/keyword_counts.json and briefly note which domains had non-zero hits overall.
   - Top 3 chores to prioritize: based strictly on total_matches from analytics/task_scores.csv (break ties by task_id ascending). For each, include task_name, frequency (from baseline), total_matches, and the domains_with_hits.
   - Artifacts: bullet the exact relative paths of all created files.

5) Orchestration and execution: create one runnable script at scripts/run_pipeline.(sh or py) that performs steps 1–3 end-to-end using only local tools and HTTP/HTTPS. Then run it so that all artifacts above actually exist under the specified paths.

Constraints
- Do not hardcode any scraped text in the repo; always derive counts from the live downloads you save under web/raw/.
- Only use the domains listed (epa.gov, energy.gov, ready.gov); no third-party APIs or headless browsers are required.
- Keep everything self-contained in this workspace using the exact relative paths specified.

Deliverables to verify
- web/raw/epa.gov.html, web/raw/energy.gov.html, web/raw/ready.gov.html
- web/text/epa.gov.txt, web/text/energy.gov.txt, web/text/ready.gov.txt
- analytics/keyword_counts.json
- analytics/task_scores.csv
- outputs/maintenance_status.md
- scripts/run_pipeline.(sh or py) that, when re-run, can regenerate the analytics files from the saved raw HTML (or re-download if missing). All file paths are relative to the workspace directory.
